corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We find that naïve strategies, which pre-train GNNs at the level of either entire graphs or individual nodes, give limited improvement and can even lead to negative transfer on many downstream tasks.

context_window.previous: We systematically study pre-training on multiple graph classification datasets.
context_window.current: We find that naïve strategies, which pre-train GNNs at the level of either entire graphs or individual nodes, give limited improvement and can even lead to negative transfer on many downstream tasks.
context_window.next: In contrast, our strategy avoids negative transfer and improves generalization sig- nificantly across downstream tasks, leading up to 9.4% absolute improvements in ROC-AUC over non-pre-trained models and achieving state-of-the-art performance for molecular property prediction and protein function prediction.

top_candidates.sentence: We mainly study Graph Isomorphism Networks (GINs) , the most expressive and state-of-the-art GNN architecture for graph-level prediction tasks.

Our systematic study suggests the following trends: Observation (1): Table shows that the most expressive GNN architecture (GIN), when pre-trained, achieves the best performance across domains and datasets.

Observation (2): As seen from the shaded cells of Table and highlighted region in the middle panel of Figure , the strong baseline strategy that performs extensive graph-level multi-task supervised pre-training of GNNs gives surprisingly limited performance gain and yields negative transfer on many downstream tasks (2 out of 8 datasets in molecular prediction, and 13 out of 40 tasks in protein function prediction).

Observation (4): From the lower half of Table and the right panel of Figure , we see that our pre-training strategy of combining graph-level multi-task supervised and node-level self-supervised pre-training avoids negative transfer across downstream datasets and achieves best performance.

Observation (5): Furthermore, from Table and the left panel of Figure , we see that our strategy gives significantly better predictive performance than the two baseline pre-training strategies as well as non-pre-trained models, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

On the HIV dataset, where a number of recent works have reported performance on the same scaffold split and using the same protocol, our best pre-trained model (ContextPred + Supervised) achieves state-ofthe-art performance.

Also, in the biology datasets, which we have built in this work, we see from the left panel of Figure that our Attribute Masking + Graph-level multi-task supervised pre-training strategy achieves the best predictive performance compared to other baseline strategies across almost all 40 downstream prediction tasks (the right panel of Figure ).
On average, our strategy improves ROC-AUC by 9.4% over non-pre-trained baseline and 5.2% over graph-level multi-task supervised pre-trained baseline, again achieving state-of-the-art performance.
There are many interesting avenues for future work.
For both domains we use the GIN architecture with some minor modifications to include edge features, as well as center node information in the protein ego-networks.
For graph-level multi-task supervised pre-training, we use a preprocessed ChEMBL dataset , containing 456K molecules with 1310 kinds of diverse and extensive biochemical assays.
Because of this, as our downstream tasks, we decided to use 8 larger binary classification datasets contained in MoleculeNet , a recently-curated benchmark for molecular property prediction.
In contrast to existing PPI datasets , our dataset is larger and spans multiple species (i.e., not only humans), which makes it a suitable benchmark for evaluating out-of-distribution prediction.
In contrast to existing PPI datasets , our dataset is larger and spans multiple species (i.e., not only humans), which makes it a suitable benchmark for evaluating out-of-distribution prediction.
For both molecular and PPI graphs, we let outer radius r 2 = r 1 + 3, and Table 2: Test ROC-AUC (%) performance of different GNN architectures with and without pre-training.

Specifically, in the chemistry datasets, we see from Table that our Context Prediction + Graph-level multi-task supervised pre-training strategy gives the most promising performance, leading to an increase in average ROC-AUC of 7.2% over non-pre-trained baseline and 4.2% over graph-level multi-task supervised pre-trained baseline.

On the HIV dataset, where a number of recent works have reported performance on the same scaffold split and using the same protocol, our best pre-trained model (ContextPred + Supervised) achieves state-ofthe-art performance.
In particular, we achieved a ROC-AUC score of 79.9%, while best-performing graph models in , , and had ROC-AUC scores of 76.3%, 77.6%, and 76.2%, respectively.
On average, our strategy improves ROC-AUC by 9.4% over non-pre-trained baseline and 5.2% over graph-level multi-task supervised pre-trained baseline, again achieving state-of-the-art performance.
However, as mentioned in Section 5.1, the extremely small dataset sizes make these benchmarks unsuitable to compare different methods in a statistically reliable way.
For example, on the MUV dataset, it took 1 hour for the non-pre-trained GNN to get 74.9% validation ROC-AUC, while it took only 5 minutes for our pre-trained GNN to get 85.3% validation ROC-AUC.
For example, on the MUV dataset, it took 1 hour for the non-pre-trained GNN to get 74.9% validation ROC-AUC, while it took only 5 minutes for our pre-trained GNN to get 85.3% validation ROC-AUC.
The dataset statistics are summarized in Table .
An effective approach to this challenge is to pre-train a model on related tasks where data is abundant, and then fine-tune it on a downstream task of interest.

In contrast, our strategy avoids negative transfer and improves generalization significantly across downstream tasks, leading up to 9.4% absolute improvements in ROC-AUC over non-pre-trained models and achieving state-of-the-art performance for molecular property prediction and protein function prediction.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
